image0.name: step_2_N_tau_and_derivative_1_20260413_122322.png
image0.caption: The differential impact of structure suppression versus thermal cooling on the 21 cm forest at redshift z=10. The cumulative number of absorbers above an optical depth $\tau$, N(>$\tau$), is strongly suppressed with increasing halo mass cutoff, M\_{cut} (left panel), as dark matter-baryon scattering eradicates the low-mass minihalos that dominate the absorber population. In contrast, the thermal cooling channel, parameterized by the cooling fraction f\_{cool}, has a negligible effect on the absorber count (middle panel) due to signal saturation in the cold intergalactic medium. The derivative of the cumulative distribution with respect to the cutoff mass, $\partial N(>\tau) / \partial M\_{cut}$ (right panel), reveals that the suppression is most pronounced at low optical depths, providing a distinct spectral fingerprint for this interaction.

image5.name: step_4_fisher_sigma_Mcut_vs_z_1_20260413_122856.png
image5.caption: Fisher forecast for the marginalized 1-$\sigma$ uncertainty on the halo mass cutoff, $\sigma(M\_{cut})$, as a function of redshift. The uncertainty increases monotonically with redshift, from $\sigma(M\_{cut}) \approx 1127 \, M\_{\odot}$ at $z=7$ to $\approx 5018 \, M\_{\odot}$ at $z=15$. This trend is driven by the decreasing number of observable background radio sources at higher redshifts, which increases Poisson noise and dominates over the enhanced intrinsic physical sensitivity to structure suppression at earlier cosmic times.

image1.name: step_2_KL_divergence_2_20260413_122322.png
image1.caption: The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, $D\_{KL}(\text{CDM} \parallel M\_{cut})$, quantifying the statistical distinguishability between the differential optical depth distributions of a standard Cold Dark Matter (CDM) model and models featuring structure suppression due to DM-baryon scattering, parameterized by a cutoff mass $M\_{cut}$. The divergence is plotted as a function of redshift for several values of $M\_{cut}$. The plot demonstrates that distinguishability increases monotonically with the suppression scale ($M\_{cut}$). For $M\_{cut} \ge 10^5 M_\odot$, the divergence also grows with redshift, indicating that the observational fingerprint of structure suppression on the 21 cm forest becomes more pronounced at earlier cosmic times.

image2.name: step_2_frac_change_heatmap_3_20260413_122322.png
image2.caption: The fractional change in the total number of 21 cm absorbers ($N\_{abs}$) relative to the standard CDM model, shown as a function of redshift $z$ and the structure suppression cutoff mass $M\_{cut}$. The plot illustrates the profound impact of the kinematic suppression of small-scale structure caused by DM-baryon scattering. Even a modest cutoff mass of $M\_{cut} = 10^4 M_\odot$ reduces the absorber count by approximately 40-50\%, while stronger interactions corresponding to $M\_{cut} \ge 5 \times 10^5 M_\odot$ eliminate over 90\% of absorbers across all redshifts. This highlights the extreme sensitivity of the 21 cm forest observable to the structural effects of DM-baryon interactions, which overwhelmingly dominate any thermal signatures.

image3.name: step_3_Nabs_vs_sigma_20260413_122538.png
image3.caption: The total number of 21 cm forest absorbers, N\_{abs}, at redshift z=10 as a function of the dark matter-baryon scattering cross-section, $\sigma_0/m\_\chi$. The left panel corresponds to a velocity-independent interaction model (n=0), while the right panel shows a Coulomb-like model (n=-4). The plot demonstrates that increasing the interaction cross-section leads to a significant reduction in the number of absorbers compared to the standard Cold Dark Matter (CDM) baseline (dashed line), a direct consequence of the suppression of minihalo formation due to collisional damping.

image4.name: step_3_sensitivity_curves_4_20260413_122634.png
image4.caption: Sensitivity of the 21 cm forest to the dark matter-baryon scattering cross-section ($\sigma\_0/m\_\chi$) as a function of redshift. The curves show the cross-section values required to suppress the total number of absorbers by 10\% (blue circles) and 20\% (orange squares) relative to the CDM prediction. Results are shown for a velocity-independent interaction ($n=0$, left panel) and a Coulomb-like interaction ($n=-4$, right panel). The probe is exceptionally sensitive to the Coulomb-like model, where the cross-section scales as $v^{-4}$, allowing constraints on $\sigma\_0/m\_\chi$ as low as $\sim 10^{-43}$ cm$^2$/GeV.
